Yes, it is.  Not though imglab, but you have to look at the interface for the objects you are editing.  Those objects allow this, you can't just do things in some functions that will cause subsequent valid uses of the object to explode.  This is all the more important since dlib is a widely used library and who knows how many uses of these objects exist in other applications.

Yes, you have to check for everything that might happen.  Or to say it another way, you can never put the object into an invalid state.  Also, the entire API for all the objects in dlib is documented in great detail (see also http://dlib.net/howto_contribute.html#1).  These functions will say things like "Calling this function removes a part.".  It will never say something like "calling this function probably removes a part, or makes your program crash depending on how the users has been moving the mouse." because that would be a terrible contract for the function.  Anyway, my point is that you can read the documented interface for these objects to find out exactly what constitutes valid behavior and "valid vs. invalid state".  

As for the index, if the parts list changes then an integer index is meaningless.  Another thing to consider is that any time the parts list changes you should probably seek back to the start of the list.  In any case, an integer index is not inherently bad, but the code you have here is constantly dealing in the string names anyway.  You might as well skip the integer part and just use the string since the code would be simpler and detection of unexpected changes more durable.
Changing anything from signed to unsigned is going to create hard to find bugs.  It would be better to use long long here.

@awestendorf, I have an idea that can work well for the publisher-acks use case. However, I don't think that we can make things better for the non-confirmation scenario, because there is no Basic.Ack/Nack in that case, so the time to receive a basic.return is unbounded.

Here is the background: Referring to the discussion in https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/rabbitmq-users/RRSFgt2A9Kw, Michael Klishin (Pivotal/RabbitMQ) is contemplating adding an extended property to the header table of the Header Frame of basic.return methods; this extended property would contain the delivery-tag value of the returned message that would permit client libraries to match it against the expected delivery tags of messages that they published. Until then, Haigha may implement the equivalent on its own by adding its own extended property with the expected delivery-tag value, and then looking it up when handling basic.return. So, depending on your perspective, haigha can either wait for the standard mechanism to come online or implement a simple equivalent that it would use with broker versions that don't have built-in support. I favor the latter.

So, this is what I was thinking for the publisher-acks use case:

1. User enables pubacks
2. When calling `channel.basic.publish()`, the user passes a `puback_cb(method, returned_msg)` (conceptual, callback signature TBD)
3. Haigha saves the delivery-tag and callback in the channel's (new) pubacks dictionary
4. On receipt of Basic.Return, the channel saves the necessary returned info, including the returned message, in the matching property in the pubacks dict, alongside the user's callback.
5. On receipt of Basic.Ack/Nack (it contains the delivery-tag in the method frame), the channel removes the matching property from the pubacks dict and calls the user's callback with the necessary info, including the returned message, if any. This works out, because \- in the pubacks scenario \- Basic.Return is guaranteed to arrive before Basic.Ack.

If you simply allow failures to propagate up, the task will fail, and the developer can decide whether the task should retry. A common python pattern is to use the module's (file's) docstring as the help string. And then
using the triple quotes to preserve newlines. Might look a bit nicer than one call to print for each line.

For example:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

'''
Usage:   neoget.py <cmd> [arg]
         -v neo4j-version: download this specific neo4j version
         -l download-url : download neo4j provided by this url
         -h              : show this help message

Example: neoget.py -v 2.3.1
         neoget.py -h
'''


def print_help():
    print(__doc__)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print_help()
```

There are 2 main reasons (in order of importance):
* Readability: Having less special cases (ifs) makes the code read better, as you don't get lost in the details, for example:

```python
def add_affiliation(author):
    if author is None:
        return

    if author == '':
        return

    affiliation = get_affiliation()
    if affiliations is None:
        return

    if affiliations == '':
        return

    if affiliations == []:
        return

    author.add_affiliation(affiliation)

# Vs
def add_affiliation(author):
    affiliation = get_affiliation()
    author.add_affiliation(affiliation)
```
    I know the example does not make much sense, but the point is that getting rid of the special cases makes it way easier to read.

* Performance: as that if is almost all the time not triggered, having to do the extra check every time is actually decreasing the performance, for example:
```python
def myfunc():
    if this_never_happens():
        return

    do_something()
    do_something_else()
```
if in that function, the if is only happening 1/10 of the times, for each 100 times it runs, it runs a total of 90*3 + 10*2 instructions, that is 290 instructions, while the same code without the if, though it does not have a shortcut:
```python
def myfunc():
    do_something()
    do_something_else()
```
runs a total of 200 instructions. It all depends on the amount of times the if is triggered of course.

For stream responses, you're expected to set `response` to an iterable:

```python
channel.SomeStreamingThing.response = [resp1, resp2, resp3]
```

For multiple invocations of the same streaming method, you should set `responses` to an iterable of iterables:

```python
channel.SomeStreamingThing.responses = [
    [resp1, resp2],
    [resp3, resp4]
]
```

This is the reason for the `response`/`responses` split.

First off, CORS is pretty weird overall.  It doesn't prevent anything outright--it just makes it a bit harder to do.  This keeps bad javascript coders from hacking too easily.

As I understand it, when making a cross-origin request the browser first sends an OPTION request to retrieve CORS headers.  I need to handle this request specifically, because the rest of the method expects a POST body and sends the results to AllenNLP.

We have an example of this kind of cosine similarity here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/9ef013d3a8abee940dbe8e2300f79f8b48cbea42/allennlp/models/semantic_parsing/wikitables/wikitables_semantic_parser.py#L191-L198

I'd say if we look at even 100 bytes (25 code points) and they are all "0 0 0 x 0 0 0 y 0 0 0 z" etc - our confidence is likely to be sky high this is UTF32.  Likewise for UTF16.  

That was my thinking - I realise it's not particularly rigorous to state this without statistical analysis of many texts however, and maybe there are counter examples I'm not aware of.

One way to improve rigour, would be to verify exceptions to zeros are actually extended characters (surrogate pairs) of UTF16 also.  For instance in UTF16 you'd be looking for quads of (D8-DB) xx (DC-DF) xx to be recognised, e.g. - https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/UTF-16#Examples

There are some other unused ranges in UTF16 and UTF32 also, so the detector could be made to flunk to zero if any of these are detected.

I'm not actually sure that `header_name` is the right description of the variable you're working with here.  If you have a header like this:

`Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' ajax.googleapis.com facebook.net ; upgrade-insecure-requests`

I think that standard terminology is that `Content-Security-Policy` is the header name, `script-src 'self' ajax.googleapis.com facebook.net ; upgrade-insecure-requests` is the header value, and `script-src` and `upgrade-insecure-requests` are either directives or substrings in that header.

So this variable might be `header_substring`, or `header_or_directive`, `apache_header_field`, or something like that?  And the function might be better named `verify_no_matching_http_header`?

If a backend supports only some operators of the graph, the user of the API (a deep learning framework) would have to do the partitioning. In the general case, the process is outlined below:
1. The user iterates operators in a model graph one-by-one, convert them to ONNX, and use `onnxGetBackendCompatibility` to check which of the operators can be offloaded to the backend.
2. The user constructs connected subgraphs of operators that can be offloaded to the backend.
3. The user queries the backend about it high-level performance characteristics using `ONNX_BACKEND_MACS_*` and `ONNX_BACKEND_MEMORY_BANDWIDTH` information queries. These data let the user build a simple roofline model of backend performance.
4. For every subgraph the user estimates time to do inference using the roofline model. The user additionally estimates time to transfer subgraph inputs to the backend using `ONNX_BACKEND_CPU_MEMORY_READ_BANDWIDTH` information query and to transfer subgraph outputs from the backend using `ONNX_BACKEND_CPU_MEMORY_WRITE_BANDWIDTH`.
5. If predicted time to transfer inputs to the backend, do inference, and transfer outputs from the backend is less than predicted time to do the inference on default engine (e.g. CPU), the user offloads the subgraph execution to the ONNX backend by calling `onnxSetGraphIO` and `onnxRunGraph`
